textPlain: MCPs are deterministic, SKILLS.md isn't. Also run.js can run arbitrarily generated Node.js code. It is a trivial vector for command injection.This might be sufficient for an independent contractor or student. It shouldn't be used in a production agent.
This was on my TODO list for the week, thanks for sharing!Now I just need to make a skill for using Jira and I can go back to the MCP-free life.
Why not just ask the agent to use Playwright via CLI? That’s what I do and it works fine. With Codex anywayEdit: oops that’s what you did too. Yes most MCP shouldn’t be used.

Thanks for the security feedback! A few clarifications:On determinism: You're right that SKILL.md instructions are interpreted by the LLM vs. MCP's protocol. However, the same non-determinism exists whenever you ask Claude to write code. Whether using this skill, writing Python scripts, or generating bash commands. The skill just provides domain-specific context.On command injection: This is designed for Claude Code's local development environment where:
  1. The user already trusts Claude with arbitrary code execution (it can write and run any code)
  2. run.js doesn't add new attack surface - Claude could write malicious Node.js and execute it directly via Bash tool
  3. The skill writes to /tmp and executes with the same permissions Claude already hasYou're absolutely right this shouldn't be used in production agents with untrusted input. The intended use case is local development/testing where you're already giving Claude full code execution privileges anyway.The tradeoff is: lower context overhead (314 lines vs continuous accessibility tree snapshots) in exchange for delegating test logic to Claude's code generation, which you're already trusting in this environment.
